questions.question: Why should you annotate a value with lowercase `string` rather than capital `String` in a type position?
options: `String` is the boxed wrapper-object interface; a `string` is assignable to it but not the reverse, so it only costs you assignability
`String` is a reserved keyword that cannot legally appear in a type annotation
`String` only matches string literals, while `string` matches any character sequence
`String` and `string` are identical, so the choice is purely a lint-style preference
questions.explanation: Lowercase `string` is the primitive; capital `String` is the JS wrapper-object interface. A `string` is assignable to `String`, but a `String` is not assignable back to `string`, so the wrapper only loses you assignability â€” there is no scenario where you need it as a value type.

questions.question: Given `const x = "on"` and `let y = "on"`, what are the inferred types of `x` and `y`?
options: `x` is `"on"` and `y` is `string`
Both are `"on"`
Both are `string`
`x` is `string` and `y` is `"on"`
questions.explanation: A `const` can never be reassigned, so the compiler safely keeps the literal type `"on"`. A `let` can be reassigned to any string, so it widens to `string` to keep the binding usable.

questions.question: Which type should you reach for at an untrusted boundary such as the result of `JSON.parse` or a `catch (e)` clause?
options: `unknown`, because it forces you to narrow before any member access, keeping the type system honest
`any`, because it lets you use the value immediately without ceremony
`object`, because parsed data is always a non-primitive reference type
`never`, because the value's shape is not yet known
questions.explanation: `unknown` is the safe top type: anything is assignable to it, but it permits no use until narrowed. `any` disables checking and spreads infectiously, so it gives zero safety at exactly the boundary where you most need it.

questions.question: `T[]` and `Array<T>` denote exactly the same type in TypeScript; choosing between them is purely a matter of style.
questions.explanation: The two spellings are identical types. `T[]` is the common repo default; `Array<T>` can read better for complex element types like `Array<() => void>`, but neither changes the meaning.

questions.question: In TypeScript's assignability lattice, which type is the top type and which is the bottom type?
options: Top is `unknown`; bottom is `never`
Top is `any`; bottom is `void`
Top is `never`; bottom is `unknown`
Top is `object`; bottom is `null`
questions.explanation: `unknown` is the top type â€” everything is assignable to it. `never` is the bottom type â€” it is assignable to every type and nothing (except `never`) is assignable to it. `any` deliberately sits outside the ordering, assignable both ways.

questions.question: What does the empty object type `{}` mean when used as an annotation, e.g. `let v: {}`?
options: Any value except `null` or `undefined`, so `42`, `"x"`, and `true` are all assignable to it
An object literal with exactly zero properties, rejecting anything with extra keys
Any non-primitive (reference) value, equivalent to the lowercase `object` type
Only the literal value `{}` constructed with empty braces
questions.explanation: `{}` means "anything that is not `null` or `undefined`," so primitives like `42` and `"x"` are accepted. It is a classic beginner trap: it does not mean "an empty object," and it is broader than the lowercase `object` type (which excludes primitives).

questions.question: What is the inferred type of `cfg`?
questions.code: const cfg = { method: "GET", path: "/users" } as const;
options: `{ readonly method: "GET"; readonly path: "/users" }`
`{ method: string; path: string }`
`{ method: "GET"; path: "/users" }` (literals kept, but mutable)
`readonly ["GET", "/users"]`
questions.explanation: `as const` makes every property deeply `readonly` and keeps each literal at its narrowest type. Without it, the properties would widen to `string` and stay mutable; it is an object literal, not an array, so it is not a tuple.

questions.subtopic: deriving a union from an array
questions.question: What is the type of `Role`?
questions.code:
const ROLES = ["admin", "editor", "viewer"] as const;
type Role = (typeof ROLES)[number];
options: `"admin" | "editor" | "viewer"`
`string`
`readonly ["admin", "editor", "viewer"]`
`string[]`
questions.explanation: `as const` makes `ROLES` a readonly tuple of string literals; indexing it with `[number]` unions the element types, yielding the string-literal union. This is the single-source-of-truth pattern for the runtime values and their type.

questions.question: Given `type Cfg = Record<string, number | string>`, which declaration both validates the value against `Cfg` and lets `port` keep the narrow type `number`?
options: `const c = { port: 8080 } satisfies Cfg;`
`const c: Cfg = { port: 8080 };`
`const c = { port: 8080 } as Cfg;`
`const c = { port: 8080 } as unknown as Cfg;`
questions.explanation: `satisfies` checks assignability to `Cfg` and then discards the type, keeping the value's own inferred type, so `c.port` stays `number`. The `: Cfg` annotation widens `port` to `number | string`; both `as` forms assert without a real structural check.

questions.question: Why does `Color["RED"]` fail to compile here?
questions.code:
enum Color { Red = "RED", Green = "GREEN" }
const x = Color["RED"];
options: String enums have no reverse mapping; only numeric enums emit a value-to-name back-map, so the string-value index is invalid
Enum members can only be accessed with dot notation, never bracket notation
`"RED"` is the value but the member is named `Red`, and member names are case-sensitive
String enums are erased at compile time, so no `Color` object exists at runtime to index
questions.explanation: Numeric enums emit a reverse map (`Color[0] === "Red"`), but string enums do not, so there is no `"RED"` -> name entry to index. The runtime object does exist for a regular string enum (only `const enum` is fully inlined).

questions.question: Under `strictNullChecks` (on by default in TS 6.0 `strict`), which of the following are true about `null` and `undefined`? (Select all that apply.)
options: `null` and `undefined` are distinct types and are not assignable to each other
An optional property `x?: T` adds `undefined` (not `null`) to the member type
`string` includes `null`, so `let s: string = null` is allowed
To allow an absent value you must opt it in explicitly via a union like `string | null`
`null` is a member of every type, just as it is without `strictNullChecks`
questions.explanation: With strictNullChecks, `null` and `undefined` are distinct non-interchangeable types, `x?: T` widens the member to `T | undefined`, and absence must be unioned in explicitly. They are NOT members of every type â€” that legacy behavior is exactly what the flag removes.

questions.question: Why is `value as T` preferred over the angle-bracket form `<T>value`?
options: The angle-bracket form is ambiguous with JSX in `.tsx` files (the parser reads `<T>` as a tag); `as` works in both
The angle-bracket form performs a runtime conversion, while `as` is compile-time only
The angle-bracket form bypasses the overlap rule, making it unsafe in all files
The angle-bracket form only works on primitive types, while `as` works on object types
questions.explanation: Both syntaxes are compile-time-only relabelings with the same overlap rules; the difference is that `<T>value` collides with JSX tag syntax in `.tsx`, so `as` is the one form that works everywhere.

questions.question: Writing `const oops = []` with no annotation infers `oops` as `any[]`, a silent `any` leak, which is why empty containers should be annotated like `const names: string[] = []`.
questions.explanation: An empty array literal with no annotation and no context infers `any[]` and does not narrow as you push elements, so the type system silently loses safety. Annotating the empty container (`const names: string[] = []`) is the recommended fix.

questions.question: Senior engineers call `any` "infectious." What does that mean in practice?
options: Member access on an `any` value yields `any` again (`danger.foo.bar()` stays `any`), so checking is silently disabled downstream
Any module that imports an `any`-typed symbol is automatically compiled in non-strict mode
`any` forces every other variable in the same scope to also become `any`
Assigning `any` to a typed variable raises a compile error that propagates to callers
questions.explanation: `any` is assignable both ways and propagates through expressions: every property access or call on an `any` is still `any`, so type checking quietly disappears downstream. It does not turn the file non-strict or force sibling variables to `any`.

questions.question: What is the inferred type of `mixed`, and why?
options: `(string | number)[]`, because best-common-type forms a union when no member is a supertype of the others
`any[]`, because the elements have different types so inference gives up
`[number, string, number]`, a tuple matching each position
`unknown[]`, because mixed element types collapse to the top type
questions.explanation: Best-common-type picks the narrowest type all elements fit; since neither `number` nor `string` is a supertype of the other, the result is the union element type `(string | number)[]`. A bare array literal is not inferred as a tuple.

questions.question: Which statements about `never` are correct? (Select all that apply.)
options: `T | never` simplifies to `T` â€” `never` is the identity element of unions
`T & never` simplifies to `never` â€” `never` absorbs in intersections
`never` is the return type of a function that always throws or loops forever
A `never` value is assignable to any type, but only a `never` value is assignable to `never`
`never` and `void` are interchangeable, since both describe functions that produce no useful value
questions.explanation: `never` vanishes from unions and absorbs in intersections, types never-returning functions, and as the bottom type is assignable to everything while accepting nothing but `never`. It is NOT interchangeable with `void`: a `void` function returns `undefined`, whereas a `never` function does not return at all.

questions.question: Which assignment is rejected by the compiler, and why?
options: `mutable = ro` is rejected, because it would let you mutate through a reference that was promised read-only
`const ro = mutable` is rejected, because a mutable array cannot be assigned to a readonly one
Both assignments are rejected, since `readonly number[]` and `number[]` are unrelated types
Neither is rejected; `readonly` is documentation only and has no effect on assignability
questions.explanation: A mutable array is assignable to a readonly array (you only drop capabilities), so `const ro = mutable` is fine. The reverse â€” `mutable = ro` â€” is rejected because it would reintroduce mutating methods on a value promised to be read-only, breaking the guarantee.

questions.question: Why is `const enum` discouraged in shared libraries?
options: Its references are inlined to literal values at emit, which single-file transpilers cannot do across files, so it breaks under `isolatedModules`
It emits a larger runtime object than a regular enum, hurting bundle size
It supports only numeric members, so string-keyed lookups are impossible
It loses the reverse mapping that consumers of a library depend on
questions.explanation: `const enum` emits no runtime object and inlines each reference to its literal value, which requires whole-program information; single-file transpilers under `isolatedModules` cannot inline a value declared in another file, so it breaks across module/declaration boundaries. That is why libraries prefer a string-literal union or a `const` object.
